oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Psittacosis
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Fronefield, Stephen
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 4 (October/November 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.by Stephen Fronefield, DVM ABC Animal and Bird Clinic, Sugarland, TexasThe causative agent of psittacosis is Chlamydia psittaci. This intracellular bacterium has zoonotic potential and as such requires special consideration when addressing the disease with pet bird owners, bird store owners and their employees, aviculturists, veterinary staff members, and veterinarians. This disease often presents as the classic “sick” bird, however information from the history and on examination may help move a diagnosis of psittacosis up on the differential diagnosis list. Managing psittacosis varies and is affected by the circumstances and number of birds afflicted or exposed to the infectious agent. Chlamydia psittaci may be shed in ocular and nasal discharges, urine, tears, oropharyngeal mucus, and in the feces of infected birds and may be detected 10 days before the onset of clinical signs. Infection occurs through inhalation or ingestion of infectious particles....Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 4 (2022): Watchbird October/November 2022; 7
